---
title: "Story Board for project: Singapore Fertility Trends: A Visual Analytics Study"
author: "G1 T16 - Cheok En Qi and Teong Sze Ching"
date: "March 12, 2026"
date-modified: "last-modified"
execute: 
  eval: true
  echo: true
  warning: false
  freeze: true
---

# Story Board Overview

Below is the story board design for our Shiny application. This will be adapted into a Shiny dashboard with interactive controls such as filters.

## 1. Home Page

The Home Page is designed to provide users with a high-level overview of Singapore’s fertility trends and the main purpose of the dashboard. It will include a short introduction to the issue of declining fertility, headline indicators such as the latest total fertility rate, and a brief explanation of the three main areas of analysis: trend exploration, Cross Correlation analysis and Panel Regression.

The purpose of this page is to orient users before they move into the detailed analytical sections. It allows users to quickly understand what the dashboard is about and what questions it can help answer.

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("data/home.png")
```

## 2. Proposed layout of the EDA page

The EDA page will allow users to interactively explore the main demographic trends in the dataset. This page will include line charts for total fertility rate, marriage, divorce, and age-specific fertility rates. Users will be able to filter the year range and select which age group they wish to display.

The purpose of this page is to help users identify broad patterns before moving into more formal analysis. For example, users can observe when fertility started declining, whether marriage and divorce indicators move in similar or opposite directions, and whether fertility has shifted towards older age groups over time.

Possible Shiny UI components for this page include: (1) sliderInput() for year range, (2) radioButtons() for selecting age bands, (3) using plotOutput() for interactive charts

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("data/EDA.png")
```

## 3. Proposed layout of the Analytical Tasks

The Analytical Tasks page will support deeper investigation into the relationship between fertility and related demographic indicators. This section will contain separate modules for cross correlation analysis and panel regression analysis.

### 3.1 Correlation Analysis

The Cross-Correlation Analysis page is designed to let users interactively examine how fertility co-moves with marriage and divorce indicators across different lags. Rather than focusing only on same-year association, this page allows users to explore whether the clearest pattern appears in the same year or after a short time shift.

The layout consists of a control panel on the left and an output panel on the right. In the control panel, users can select the indicator to examine, choosing between Marriage, Divorce, or Both for comparison. Users can also adjust the maximum lag to define the lag window shown in the analysis, and filter the year range to focus on a selected analysis period.

The output panel displays two main elements. First, a cross-correlation plot shows how the correlation between fertility and the selected indicator changes across the lag window. When both indicators are selected, the plot compares the lag patterns for marriage and divorce in the same view. Second, a lag values table displays the exact cross-correlation values for each lag within the selected range. 

This page is intended to support exploratory analysis of the overall lag structure in the aggregate yearly series. It helps users identify whether the strongest alignment with fertility occurs in the same year or at a shifted lag, and provides a clear bridge to the panel regression section, where the same-year relationship is examined more formally.

Possible Shiny UI components for this page include: sliderInput() for year range, radioButtons() for indicator selection, sliderInput() for maximum lag, plotOutput() for the cross-correlation plot, DTOutput() for the lag values table

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("data/corr.png")
```

### 3.2 Panel Regression Analysis

The Panel Regression Analysis page is designed to let users examine the relationship between fertility, marriage, and divorce more formally after the overall lag pattern has been identified in the cross-correlation section. Since the cross-correlation analysis showed that the clearest co-movement for both indicators appears at lag 0, this page focuses on the same-year specification in the panel regression model.

The layout consists of a control panel on the left and an output panel on the right. In the control panel, users can filter the year range to focus on a selected analysis period. This allows the panel regression results to be re-estimated over different time spans while keeping the same overall modelling structure.

The output panel displays two main elements. First, a fixed-effects coefficient plot shows the estimated same-year coefficients for marriage and divorce, allowing users to compare the direction and relative size of the relationships. Second, a summary table reports the coefficient estimates, standard errors, and p-values from the fixed-effects model.

Possible Shiny UI components for this page include: (1) sliderInput() for year range, (2) plotOutput() for the fixed-effects coefficient plot, (3) DTOutput() for the regression summary table.

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("data/regression.png")
```

## 4. Package Selection Justification

To support the development of the Shiny application, we selected packages based on clarity of output, interpretability, ease of integration with Shiny, and suitability for the project’s time-series and panel-data structure.

For visualisation, we used ggplot2 as the main charting library because it produces clear and consistent visual output, is highly customisable, and integrates well with Shiny through plotOutput(). It is well suited for presenting demographic trends over time, age-group fertility patterns, coefficient comparison charts, and cross-correlation summaries in a structured and readable format.

For data preparation and transformation, we used functions from the tidyverse, especially dplyr and tidyr, because they support clear and efficient data manipulation. These packages were useful for reshaping yearly and age-group datasets, filtering user-selected inputs, and preparing panel data for modelling.

For interactive tabular output, we used the DT package because it allows model summaries and cross-correlation results to be displayed in a more readable and user-friendly table format within the Shiny app.

For cross-correlation analysis, we used base R’s ccf() together with TSstudio for time-series visualisation. These tools were selected because they are appropriate for examining lagged co-movement between fertility and demographic indicators, which better matches the time-series nature of the data than ordinary same-year correlation analysis.

For panel-data analysis, we referred to both ExPanDaR and plm. The final panel regression models in this project were implemented using plm, which provided a more direct and transparent setup for the fixed-effects and random-effects specifications used in the Shiny application.

To support panel model evaluation, we also used lmtest and sandwich. These packages are useful for hypothesis testing and robust inference, including model comparison and coefficient testing.

Overall, these packages were chosen because they support a Shiny application that is methodologically aligned with the project’s objectives, while keeping the outputs interpretable and suitable for interactive exploration.
